text.title: Data Analysis — Core Concepts and Process
text.summary: Data analysis components, five-step process, data element attributes, data modeling levels, six quality dimensions, and data rules taxonomy.

section.title: What Data Analysis Examines
section.p: Data Analysis in the BA context covers five questions:

list.item: What data the business needs — reports, screens, input, calculated, stored, shared.
How data is structured — entities, attributes, relationships.
Where data comes from and goes — sources, targets, integration paths.
Data quality requirements — accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness.
Data relationships — cardinality, business rules, constraints.

rule.statement: For each data category, document the question it answers and its role in the system before defining attributes.
rule.rationale: Starting with attributes before understanding the data's purpose produces dictionaries that capture fields nobody uses and miss fields everyone needs.

list.item: What information do users need? — Reports and screens (consumed data).
What data is captured? — Input data (created by users or external systems).
What data is calculated? — Derived data (computed from other fields).
What data is stored persistently? — Domain entities.
What data is shared with other systems? — Integration data with defined contracts.

section.title: Step 2: Define Data Elements
section.p: For each attribute, document all seven characteristics:

list.item: Name — clear, unique, business-facing name (not column name).
Definition — business meaning in plain language.
Type — text, number, date, boolean, enumeration.
Format — specific format rules (e.g., ISO 8601 dates, E.164 phone numbers).
Values — valid values or ranges; enumeration members.
Source — where the data originates.
Owner — who is responsible for data quality.

rule.statement: Every number cited in a requirements document must carry a definition footnote: metric name, definition, source system, snapshot date.
rule.rationale: Numbers travel without their definitions; within weeks a cited metric becomes folklore that different teams interpret differently, producing conflicting implementations.

list.item: Conceptual — high-level entities and relationships. Audience: business stakeholders. No attributes.
Logical — detailed attributes, data types, relationships, and business rules. Audience: BA and developers.
Physical — tables, columns, indexes, constraints. Audience: database developers. Out of BA scope to author; BA reviews for alignment with logical model.

rule.statement: Assess all six quality dimensions before defining data requirements. Missing dimensions produce requirements that assume data quality the system does not have.
rule.rationale: Systems built on inaccurate or inconsistent data inherit the data's defects; post-launch data quality remediation is significantly more expensive than pre-spec assessment.

list.item: Accuracy — correct values. Measure: error rate. Target threshold: less than 2%.
Completeness — all required data present. Measure: missing value percentage.
Consistency — same values across systems. Measure: conflict count between sources.
Timeliness — current and available. Measure: age of data against business SLA.
Validity — conforms to business rules. Measure: invalid value percentage.
Uniqueness — no duplicates. Measure: duplicate rate.
Validation — Email must contain @; phone must match format.
Derivation — Total = Quantity x Price; Age = today - birth date.
Default — Status defaults to "New" on creation.
Constraint — Start date must be before end date.
Uniqueness — CustomerID is unique; Email is unique per account.
